entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-4]
entry.content:
Connecting CAP application to HANA Cloud and Deploying  to Cloud Foundry

In Part 3 , we created the SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP and mapped it to our Cloud Foundry space.

In this Part, we will:

- Update package.json with the correct CDS profiles for each environment
- Run cds add hana to wire the CAP project for HANA Cloud deployment
- Build the MTA archive using mbt build
- Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
- Verify the deployment and explore the HANA tables in DB Explorer
- Run the app locally in hybrid mode — connected to the deployed HANA instance

PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed before proceeding:

- Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) — logged in to your BTP CF environment: cf login
- MBT (Multi-Target Application Build Tool): npm install -g mbt
- @sap/cds-dk (already installed from Part 1)


STEP 1 — UPDATE PACKAGE.JSON WITH CDS PROFILES

The package.json in the CAP project already has a "cds" section that defines how the app connects to the database in each environment. We need to make sure the three profiles are correctly configured:

development → uses SQLite (local, in-file database — for cds watch)
hybrid → uses SAP HANA Cloud (local app, cloud database — for testing)
production → uses SAP HANA Cloud (fully deployed on CF)

Update or verify the "cds" section in your package.json:

{
  "name": "incident-management",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Incident Management CAP Application",
  "repository": "<Add your repository here>",
  "license": "UNLICENSED",
  "private": true,
  "dependencies": {
    "@cap-js/hana": "^2",
    "@sap/cds": "^9",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "@sap/xssec": "^4",
    "express": "^5"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@cap-js/sqlite": "^2",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "cds-plugin-ui5": "^0.17.0"
  },
  "cds": {
    "requires": {
      "[development]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "sqlite"
        }
      },
      "[hybrid]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      },
      "[production]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "watch": "cds watch",
    "watch-incidents": "cds watch --open incidents/index.html?sap-ui-xx-viewCache=false --livereload false"
  }
}

WHY THREE PROFILES?
This pattern lets you use the same codebase across all environments without changing any code — just switch the profile.

SQLite keeps local development fast and offline.

Hybrid lets you test against real HANA data without a full CF deployment.

Production is used when the app runs on Cloud Foundry.

 

STEP 2 — RUN cds add hana

Run the following command in the terminal from inside the project folder:

cds add hana

This single command does several important things to prepare your CAP project for SAP HANA Cloud deployment:

- Adds the @cap-js/hana dependency to package.json (HANA database adapter)
- Generates or updates the mta.yaml file with the correct MTA module definitions for CF deployment
- Adds an HDI container resource definition to mta.yaml
- Configures the production CDS profile to use hana-cloud as the db kind

After running this command, your mta.yaml will define three components:

1. incident-management-srv (Node.js module — the CAP OData service)
- Deployed as a CF application
- Provides: srv-api (the service URL consumed by the Fiori app later)
- Requires: incident-management-db (the HDI container)

2. incident-management-db-deployer (hdb module — the schema deployer)
- A one-time deployer app that pushes the CDS schema to HANA
- Runs during deployment then stops (Stopped state is normal)
- Requires: incident-management-db

3. incident-management-db (resource — the HDI container)
- Type: com.sap.xs.hdi-container
- Service: hana, Plan: hdi-shared
- This is the actual schema container created inside your HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) that was set up in Part 3 


STEP 3 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the MBT build command to compile the project into a deployable archive:

mbt build

This command:
- Reads the mta.yaml configuration
- Runs `npm install` and `npx cds build --production` to compile the CDS model
- Packages all modules into a single .mtar archive file

The output archive is created at:

mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

 

STEP 4 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Make sure you are logged in to CF and targeting the correct space:

cf login -a <api endpoint>
cf target -o <your-org> -s dev

Now deploy the MTA archive:

cf deploy mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

CF will:
1. Create the HDI container service instance (incident-management-db)
2. Deploy and run the db-deployer to push the schema to HANA
3. Start the CAP service application (incident-management-srv)

Once deployment completes, navigate to your CF space in BTP Cockpit:

Trial Home →   f030ff54trial   → trial   → dev → Applications

You will see two applications in the "dev" space:

 

STEP 5 — VERIFY THE HDI CONTAINER INSTANCE

Navigate to:

Space: dev → Services → Instances

You should see the HDI container service instance that was automatically created during deployment:

 This HDI container lives inside the SHC_DB HANA Cloud instance we created in Part 3. CAP automatically created it during the cf deploy step.

 

STEP 6 — EXPLORE THE HANA TABLES IN DB EXPLORER

Now let's verify the schema was deployed correctly by exploring the tables in SAP HANA Database Explorer.

Open HANA Cloud Central → click "Open" on the SHC_DB instance →
select "SAP HANA Database Explorer".

In Database Explorer, the left panel shows your SHC-DB instance connected as DBADMIN. To view the HDI container tables, click the "+" (Add Instance) icon.

 

Select "HDI Container" as the Instance Type, filter by your org (f030ff54trial) and space (dev).

Select "incident-management-db" and click OK.

 

The HDI container is now added to the left panel. Expand it to see the Tables folder. You will find all the CDS entities have been deployed as HANA tables.

Click on SAP_CAPIRE_INCIDENTS_INCIDENTS to inspect its structure:


STEP 7 — RUNNING IN HYBRID MODE

Now that the CAP app is deployed and the HDI container exists in BTP, you can run your local development server connected directly to the cloud HANA database. This is the "hybrid" mode — your app runs locally but reads and writes real HANA Cloud data.

This is very useful for testing local code changes against production data before deploying again.

Step 1 — Bind your local project to the HDI container service instance:

cds bind -2 incident-management-db:key

What this does:
- Connects to Cloud Foundry and retrieves the service key for the "incident-management-db" HDI container instance
- Creates a service key on BTP (if one doesn't exist)
- Saves the HANA connection credentials (host, schema, user, password) into .cdsrc-private.json under the "hybrid" profile
- The .cdsrc-private.json file is local only — never commit it to git

Step 2 — Start the server in hybrid mode:

cds watch --profile hybrid

The terminal confirms the app is now connected to the real HANA Cloud database, showing:
- The HANA host and schema ID
- Driver: com.sap.db.jdbc.Driver (JDBC connection to HANA)
- Both ProcessorService and AdminService are being served

Your app on localhost:4004 now reads and writes directly to the HANA Cloud instance in BTP — the Fiori preview will show and save real data.


SUMMARY

In Part 4, we successfully:

✔ Configured package.json with three CDS profiles: development (SQLite), hybrid (local + HANA Cloud), production (CF + HANA)
✔ Ran cds add hana to wire the project for HANA Cloud and generate mta.yaml
✔ Built the deployable MTA archive using mbt build
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
✔ Verified the two CF apps: incident-management-srv (Started) and incident-management-db-deployer (Stopped — expected)
✔ Confirmed the HDI container instance was created with 2 bindings
✔ Explored the HANA tables and seed data in SAP HANA Database Explorer
✔ Connected the local app to the HANA Cloud instance using hybrid mode

The CAP backend is now live on Cloud Foundry and backed by SAP HANA Cloud.
In Part 5 , we will build the Fiori Elements UI application and connect it to this deployed CAP service.

entry.title: Why Most SAP Integration Migrations Fail— and How IntegrateHub Is Built to Prevent That
entry.content:
Over the years of working closely with our integration and modernization teams at Crave InfoTech, I have sat in on enough post-project debriefs to notice a pattern. The migrations that ran over budget, over schedule, or into operational disruption almost never failed because of the technology. They failed in the planning phase — specifically, because the complexity of the existing landscape was not properly understood before execution began.

That observation is what this post is really about. Not just what SAP Integration Suite offers, but what it actually takes to get there — and why tooling like IntegrateHub exists to address the gaps that standard migration approaches leave open.

Organizations that have completed this journey are reporting maintenance costs down by 40% and system performance improved by 50%. IT teams have visibility they did not have before. Compliance teams have stopped manually assembling reports across multiple systems. Those outcomes are real — but they sit on the other side of a migration program, and how that program is structured determines whether you arrive there on time, on budget, and without operational disruption.

What You Are Actually Moving Away From — and Why It Matters

SAP PI/PO landscapes accumulate years of custom logic: Java mappings, ABAP objects, and User Defined Functions written to solve specific problems at a specific point in time. Hundreds of interfaces, sometimes thousands, each with its own adapter configurations and dependencies and none of it converts automatically.

From what I have seen across the programs our teams have run, one of the most consistent early mistakes is the interface count. Teams count the visible interfaces and miss the rest — deprecated adapters, decade-old Java mappings, ABAP custom code that should have been retired releases ago. None of it shows up in a manual count. Scope gets underestimated, and the project collides with reality during detailed analysis.

SAP Neo compounds this with architectural limitations that block multi-cloud strategies and modern integration patterns. SAP has been steering customers toward Cloud Foundry and SAP BTP for some time now, and that direction is not changing.

Then there is the third-party middleware reality. TIBCO, MuleSoft, Boomi, BizTalk, IBM MQ, WebSphere, webMethods, Sterling — each has its own object model, mapping language, and deployment architecture. Moving from any of these to SAP Integration Suite is not a like-for-like conversion. It requires analysis, re-platforming, and in many cases, redesign. SAP's standard migration tools cover SAP-to-SAP scenarios well. They do not cover third-party middleware — SAP legally cannot build accelerators for competing platforms. Every organization migrating from those platforms faces that gap directly, and it is where migration programs lose the most time.

What SAP Integration Suite Offers That Legacy Platforms Don't

SAP Integration Suite is SAP's cloud-based integration platform built on SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform). It includes Cloud Integration (CPI) for message-based and API-based integrations, API Management for governing and exposing APIs, Event Mesh for event-driven architecture, and Integration Advisor for accelerating B2B/EDI mapping.

The shift from SAP PI/PO to Integration Suite is not just a platform upgrade. It is a move from an on-premise, batch-oriented, custom-code-heavy integration model to a cloud-native, API-first, event-driven one. That shift enables real-time data exchange, better monitoring, cleaner system architecture, and a foundation that supports AI, automation, and multi-cloud strategies.

Getting there means converting, rebuilding, and validating everything your teams have built and customized over the years — interfaces, mappings, ABAP objects, Java code. That work does not disappear because the destination platform is better. It just becomes more manageable with the right tooling 

Why the Gap in Tooling Is the Real Problem

When I look at where migration programs most commonly lose time, it comes down to three things: interface discovery that misses the real scope, manual conversion work that takes far longer than estimated, and testing cycles that are not designed for the complexity of non-1:1 integration flows.

The third-party middleware gap is particularly acute. Because SAP cannot build conversion accelerators for competing platforms, organizations migrating from TIBCO, MuleSoft, Boomi, or similar systems have historically had no choice but to do that work manually. Any timeline estimate for it is, candidly, a guess — and it is usually an optimistic one.

That is the specific problem IntegrateHub by Crave InfoTech was built to solve.

How IntegrateHub Is Built to Handle That Complexity

IntegrateHub operates as a single end-to-end platform across 10 modules, each addressing a specific stage or failure point in the migration lifecycle. It runs on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Environment and was certified by SAP SE in February 2026 under the BTPEXT-GenAI integration scenario, confirming its use of generative AI hub in SAP AI Core and compliance with SAP's Responsible AI requirements.

Here is what each module does and why it matters:

  •       Company & Set-Up — Establishes the migration environment, user roles, system connections, and integration parameters. Getting this right at the start prevents governance problems downstream.
  •       Assess — This is the module I consider most critical. It discovers, analyzes, and classifies existing interfaces from SAP PI/PO and third-party middleware automatically. What would take a team of consultants several weeks of manual inventory work is reduced to a structured, data-driven baseline. The output feeds directly into the migration plan.
  •       Migrate — Auto-generates SAP Integration Suite iFlows from legacy interface designs. This is not a copy-paste operation — it converts the structural logic of existing interfaces into Integration Suite-compliant designs, materially reducing manual build effort.
  •       Test — Simulates integrations, performs regression testing, and validates migrated flows, including file-based interfaces and non-1:1 flows. A built-in comparison engine confirms data consistency between source and migrated flows before any go-live decision.
  •       Document — Generates technical documentation, migration reports, and audit trails directly from Integration Suite configurations. In regulated industries, this is not optional — and relying on individuals to produce it manually is a compliance risk.
  •       Conversion — Transforms Java programs and UDFs into Groovy scripts, and converts XSLT mappings into Integration Suite-compliant artifacts. For organizations with large PI/PO landscapes, this is the highest-effort activity in a manual migration.
  •       Optimization — Applies AI-driven analysis to post-migration flows, identifying redundancies and improving performance. The goal is an integration landscape that is operationally better, not just technically equivalent to what existed before.
  •       Dashboard — Tracks migration progress, complexity, readiness, connector coverage, and open risks in a single real-time view. Most migration programs I have observed suffer from a visibility problem. This module addresses it directly.
  •       iFlow AutoGen — Generates integration flows using natural language prompts, reducing dependency on specialist availability during build phases. This is where the generative AI certification comes into active use.

Together, these modules deliver up to 50% reduction in migration effort and timeline — with measurably higher accuracy in assessment, faster validation cycles, and consistent documentation across all artifacts.

Watch IntegrateHub video here. 

SAP-to-SAP vs. Third-Party-to-SAP: Not the Same Migration

One distinction I want to be direct about is that these are not the same migration, and they should not be treated as such.

SAP-to-SAP migrations (PI/PO or Neo to Integration Suite) involve ABAP object assessment, Java mapping migration, UDF and adapter module conversion, and post-implementation documentation. IntegrateHub handles all of this within a single program.

Third-party-to-SAP migrations are where most standard tooling runs out. Because SAP cannot build accelerators for competing platforms, whereas, Crave InfoTech has built IntegrateHub, which includes purpose-built migration tooling for TIBCO, MuleSoft, Boomi, BizTalk, IBM MQ, WebSphere, webMethods, and Sterling — with dedicated migration tools for Boomi and MuleSoft available today. Both tracks run within the same migration program, without splitting the engagement or the tooling.

Migration That Modernizes, Not Just Moves

This is a distinction I think matters and is worth stating clearly. A straight conversion replicates your existing landscape in a new environment. That is not what IntegrateHub produces.

Modernization architecture is embedded into the migration itself — interfaces are rebuilt with API-first and event-driven patterns from the start, not retrofitted later. The output supports clean-core alignment, multi-cloud readiness, Edge Integration Cell for hybrid and regulated environments, AI-driven mappings, and AI agent compatibility. You move to SAP Integration Suite with an integration landscape designed for where SAP is going and not with a cloud-hosted copy of what you had.

What This Looks Like at Enterprise Scale

Scale is where any migration approach gets tested. Across 50+ programs in 20 countries — from landscapes with 22 interfaces to over 3,700 — our teams have worked in life sciences, pharmaceuticals, oil and gas, retail, manufacturing, and public sector environments where integration failures carry direct business and regulatory consequences.

Published programs include an American pharmaceutical MNC, a leading life sciences enterprise, and a major U.S. retailer — each migrating to SAP Integration Suite with their own legacy complexity and business continuity requirements.

Where to Start

If you are running on SAP PI/PO, SAP Neo, or non-SAP middleware and evaluating your path to SAP Integration Suite, the most practical starting point is a free assessment. Two phases, no architectural changes required, no financial commitment — and you come away with a factual picture of your landscape, complexity, and what a realistic migration program would look like.

Most migrations that fail do not fail because the technology was wrong. They fail because the complexity was not understood clearly enough before execution started. That is the problem worth solving first.

IntegrateHub is available on the SAP Store and is a product of Crave InfoTech LLC. It is listed on the SAP Certified Solutions Directory at www.sap.com/csd.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud - You Want to Export to Excel? Not Today. 🚫📊
entry.content:
A Step-by-Step Guide to Gently (but Firmly) Stopping Your SAC Users from Living Their Best Excel Life

We've all been there. You've spent weeks building a beautiful SAP Analytics Cloud Story. The charts are crisp, the filters are elegant, and the HR data is safely governed inside SAC's secure environment.

And then... someone discovers the Export button.

This post is your survival guide. A lovingly passive-aggressive walkthrough of every trick your users will try to get your data into Excel — and exactly how to stop them. Think of it as a game. They move. You block. They move again. You block again. Eventually, everyone goes home.

Let's begin.

🎮Level 1: "I'll just export the whole story!"

Our user, let's call him Excel-Ernst, opens the SAC story, clicks File, and grins. "I'll just export this whole thing to Excel," he thinks. Easy.

Sorry, Ernst. SAC doesn't allow a full story export to Excel or CSV. The Export Story dialog only offers PDF, PowerPoint, or Google Slides. Not quite what he had in mind.

But wait — Ernst can still see the export button in the toolbar. And that's already too much. Go to View Time Toolbar Settings and disable the export option there. Do this for every access point where the export functionality can be reached. Yes, all of them.

 ✅ Block achieved. Ernst doesn't see a export functionality for the Story. He moves on.

🎮Level 2: "Fine. I'll just export this one chart."

Ernst discovers the three-dot menu on a chart widget. "More Options → Export." A friendly little dialog appears: Export Chart to CSV. He is delighted.

By default, every chart in SAC can be exported to CSV. And tables? Tables can go straight to Excel. Right there. One click.

Here's how you stop him:

Option A (the nuclear option): Disable all widget interaction via the Builder panel → View Mode → Disable Interaction. This kills export — but also kills drilling, sorting, and basically everything else. It's the "touch nothing" option. Use with caution.

Option B (the surgical strike, recommended): Go to the Styling panel → Quick Menus and uncheck Export. This removes only the export option from the widget's context menu, leaving all other interactions intact. You can even do it per widget, so a summary chart stays interactive while the detail table does not.

✅Block achieved. Ernst's export option has vanished. He squints at the screen. He tries right-clicking. Nothing. He sighs.

🎮Level 3: "What about the Data Analyzer?"

Ernst is getting creative. He found the Data Analyzer — that nifty exploration tool accessible from certain widgets. And inside the Data Analyzer? A beautiful toolbar with Export Excel, Export CSV, and Export PDF buttons, lined up like a buffet.

This one requires a two-step fix:

  1. In Story Details, turn off "Enable Data Analyzer for all supported widgets" under Data Exploration Settings.
  2. In the Table Properties of individual tables, also disable the Data Analyzer access — because the story-level toggle alone doesn't catch everything.

Yes, both. We know.

 ✅ Block achieved. The Data Analyzer access from a Story is gone. Ernst feels personally attacked. He opens a new browser tab.

🎮Level 4: "I'll open it directly in the Excel Add-In."

Ernst has done his research. He discovered that with the right table settings, there's an option in the widget menu: "Open in Excel Add-In." He activates it. He is unstoppable.

First: make sure this option is not enabled in the table properties. It has to be explicitly activated, so as long as nobody turns it on, it won't appear.

But here's the uncomfortable truth — even if you lock down every story, every widget, and every export button: if a user has access to the underlying SAC or Datasphere model, they can still use the Excel Add-In independently to query that model directly. The Add-In doesn't create a new authorization layer. It simply respects existing SAC and Datasphere permissions.

So if the story access is based on a Datasphere analytical model, the user technically also has Add-In access to that model. "Story access yes, Excel access no" is not a supported configuration.

What you can do:

  • Remove Space Files permissions from consumer roles in Datasphere by creating a custom Role. This prevents users from actively browsing and selecting models in the SAC Excel Add In or the Data Analyzer in SAC, effectively disabling self-service Excel analysis — while still allowing them to consume SAC stories normally.
  • Centrally control Add-In deployment via Microsoft 365 administration. The Add-In should not be available to everyone — only to users with a documented business need for Excel-based SAC analysis.

✅Partial block achieved. Ernst can no longer pick models freely in the SAC Excel Add In. He mutters something about governance being "a bit much."

🏁The Boss Level: Design

Here's the dirty secret that all of the above controls are working around: if employee-level detail records are visible in a table, the data is already exposed. Export buttons or not, a determined user can copy-paste. Screenshot. Memorize (okay, probably not that last one).

The strongest control isn't a setting. It's design.

Design Principle What it means

Aggregated KPIs onlyShow sums, averages, counts — not individual records
Summary firstFor example HR dashboards show overviews; detail is the exception
Detail by exceptionIndividual-level data only in separate, access-controlled stories
Disable Data Analyzer by defaultDon't expose ad-hoc exploration unless explicitly needed

If sensitive data isn't in the story, it can't be exported from the story. Simple, elegant, and no amount of right-clicking will change it.

Final Words

Ernst is still out there. He's resourceful, well-intentioned, and genuinely just trying to do his job. The goal isn't to make his life impossible — it's to make accidental data leakage impossible, while still giving him what he legitimately needs.

Use the controls. Design defensively. And maybe send Ernst a nicely formatted PDF instead.

He'll be fine.

Have questions about SAC governance, role design, or Datasphere model permissions? Drop them in the comments below!

AI Usage Disclosure:
Generative AI was used to support language refinement and structuring of the article.
All technical content, validation, screenshots, and implementation details were created and verified by the author.

entry.title: Is SAP Missing a Bigger Opportunity in the SME Market?
entry.content:
The ERP market is changing faster than ever.

A few years ago, businesses mainly focused on digitization. Today, the conversation has completely shifted toward AI, automation, cloud ecosystems, and intelligent operations.

Every major IT company is now positioning itself around AI in some way. From startups to enterprise giants, everyone is racing toward the next innovation cycle.

But in between all this innovation, I feel one important challenge is often overlooked — ERP awareness and accessibility for mid-sized businesses.

And honestly, this is where I think SAP has both a massive opportunity and a challenge.

There’s no doubt that SAP S/4HANA remains one of the most powerful ERP platforms in the enterprise world. It dominates large-scale enterprise operations with strong capabilities across finance, supply chain, manufacturing, analytics, and business transformation.

At the same time, the market perception of SAP is still heavily associated with:

  • Large enterprises
  • High implementation cost
  • Complex transformation projects
  • Long deployment cycles

Because of this, many SMEs and mid-market companies often move toward lighter ERP platforms such as Odoo or other flexible ERP ecosystems before even exploring SAP seriously.

Now yes — SAP has already made strong moves with:

  • SAP BTP
  • SAP Business One
  • Cloud-first innovation
  • AI integrations
  • Low-code/no-code capabilities

And SAP BTP is becoming a major innovation layer for AI, integrations, analytics, workflow automation, and clean-core architecture.

But I still personally feel there is a larger awareness gap in the SME market.

Many growing businesses still see SAP as:

“Too enterprise-focused for us.”

Meanwhile, those same companies are actively investing in:

  • Automation
  • AI tools
  • Analytics
  • Connected business systems
  • Real-time reporting

Which are actually areas where SAP can deliver tremendous value.

The interesting part is: mid-sized companies today want enterprise-grade capabilities without enterprise-level complexity.

And maybe this is where the future conversation becomes important.

Can SAP simplify adoption further for the SME ecosystem while still maintaining the power of S/4HANA and BTP?

Because the demand clearly exists.

Especially now, when businesses are looking for:

  • AI-ready ERP environments
  • Scalable cloud architecture
  • Better integrations
  • Faster decision-making
  • Real-time operational visibility

Personally, I believe SAP’s future growth opportunity is not only in large enterprises — but also in making intelligent ERP ecosystems more approachable for growing businesses.

I may be wrong here, and I’m still learning in this space myself, but I’d genuinely love to hear different perspectives from the SAP community.

What are your thoughts?

Do you think SAP should focus more aggressively on the SME and mid-market segment in the coming years?

entry.title: Re: SAP CC database migration from Oracle to HANA
entry.content:
Hi Doris,

Thanks for your message.
SAP CC has no standard procedure for migrating to a different database.
Yet, in the past, we've recommended users to design their own procedure, based on:

  • their specific constraints (e.g., source vs. destination DB vendor, available SQL clients, amount and type of data to migrate, etc.)
  • SAP CC's official procedure for a "system copy"

The general principle is to install the new CC system normally (i.e., using SAPinst), and then export the existing table rows from the old database to reimport them into HANA using the adequate SQL client(s).
Among the reasons for adjusting the standard steps:

  • Users are normally expected to migrate between two identical CC systems (same version, but also same database vendor).
    In the present case, you'll have to decide on the best suited SQL client separately, for each system.
  • If the procedure seems overly complex for your specific situation, you might prefer migrating (part of) the database using other means.
    For example, for some types of objects, you might be interested in fetching and recreating the data using the API's: that's slower for large volumes, and it'd also require some custom development, but it may help in some cases (especially if your data comes from an external source that is already integrated with the API).
    Other than this, you may even want to recreate some objects manually, or to export and reimport them using Cockpit/Core tool.

Here are some documents related with this topic:

  • "system copy" procedure for SAP CC:
    https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-cc/install-guide/copying-sap-cc-core-server-system-and-its-database
  • pitfall regarding the order of the columns between the source and the destination:
    https://me.sap.com/notes/3057000

These are just generic guidelines, feel free to let us know if you need more precise advice (we also recommend to involve SAP Center of Expertise if you need assistance with the actual implementation).

Best regards.

François
SAP Convergent Charging Support

entry.title: Modernizing Healthcare ERP Systems (SAP S/4HANA) to Strengthen Operations and Support Better Care
entry.content:
Why I Took This Project

When I was asked to lead this SAP S/4HANA rollout for a U.S.-based medical device company, the decision wasn't difficult. Medical devices directly affect patients. When the systems behind those devices work well — when inventory is accurate, when compliance is airtight, when the right product reaches the right place at the right time — patients benefit. That connection between back-end ERP operations and real patient outcomes was what made this project meaningful to me from day one.

What I didn't fully appreciate when I said yes was just how complex the journey from 2020 to 2022 would be. Multiple U.S. business units. A fresh S/4HANA template built from scratch. Nine external system integrations. A 50-person team. SOX compliance requirements. And the kind of data challenges that don't reveal themselves until you're deep in System Integration Testing wondering what just broke and why.

I'm glad I took it. But there are things I would do differently — and that's really what this post is about.

What We Were Actually Trying to Do

The organization had multiple U.S. business units operating independently, each with its own processes, systems, and way of doing things. The goal was straightforward in concept: bring all of them onto a single SAP S/4HANA platform, unit by unit, using a fresh template built specifically for this program.

Straightforward in concept. Genuinely complex in execution.

We chose a phased rollout — one business unit at a time — rather than a big-bang go-live. That was the right call. It gave us the ability to learn from each phase and improve the template before the next unit went live. But it also meant we were running parallel environments for extended periods, synchronizing data between legacy systems and the live SAP landscape throughout the program.

The full module scope included:

  • MM (Materials Management) — purchasing and inventory across all business units
  • EWM (Extended Warehouse Management) — serialized device tracking across U.S. sites
  • QM (Quality Management) — batch records, inspection lots, quality notifications
  • SD (Sales & Distribution) — order management across units with different customer bases
  • FI/CO (Finance & Controlling) — SOX-controlled financial reporting and cost accounting
  • MDG (Master Data Governance) — material, vendor, and customer master governance
  • SAP IBP (Integrated Business Planning) — demand and supply planning
  • SAP GRC Access Control — Segregation of Duties, reviewed by Big 4 auditors at each phase

That's a lot of moving parts. And every one of them had to connect to nine external systems simultaneously.

The Integration Reality

I want to be honest about this part because I don't think it gets talked about enough in SAP project write-ups.

The integration landscape for this program included:

External System Function
Model NRevenue management and pricing
SalesforceCRM and customer management
Oracle CPQConfigure, price, quote
ServiceMaxField service management
CoupaProcurement and supplier management
VertexTax calculation
SAP CommissionsSales commission management
LanveraDocument output and customer communications
Power BIReporting and executive dashboards

Nine integrations running through SAP Middleware and PI. Each one a data contract between two systems that don't naturally speak the same language.

Here's what I learned: integrations don't break because of technology. They break because of data. Unexpected data formats, missing fields, values that exist in one system but mean something completely different in another — these are what cause MW and PI failures. We had failures during SIT cycles that stopped testing in its tracks. In those moments the instinct is to look at the technical layer first. More often than not, the root cause was data.

What helped us move through those failures was thorough, structured testing — not just happy path scenarios but deliberately testing edge cases, bad data, and boundary conditions before go-live. Every time we skipped that discipline, we paid for it later.

If you're heading into a multi-integration SAP rollout: test the data, not just the interface.

The Worst Moments — and What They Taught Me

I'm not going to pretend this program ran smoothly. There were difficult periods, particularly during SIT cycles when multiple deliverables failed close together. Those moments are genuinely hard — the team is under pressure, stakeholders are watching, and the temptation is to find someone to blame or to rush fixes through without fully understanding the root cause.

What I found in those moments was that the answer was almost always the same: go back to the data.

A failed deliverable in SIT is rarely a configuration problem in isolation. It's usually a configuration built on assumptions about data that turned out to be wrong. When we took the time to trace failures back to their data root cause — rather than patching the symptom — we fixed things properly and didn't see them break again in the next test cycle.

That discipline is harder than it sounds when you're three weeks from a phase go-live and the pressure is real. But it's worth it.

The Lesson That Changed How I Work

Before this program, I thought about SAP implementations in terms of modules and workstreams. MM had its requirements. QM had its requirements. Integration had its requirements. Each track did its work and they came together at the end.

This project changed that completely.

I now think about SAP programs from an organizational perspective first — not a module perspective.

What does that mean practically? It means that when a business unit stakeholder describes a requirement, my first question is no longer "which module owns this?" It's "what does the data behind this requirement actually look like, and does it behave the same way across every system that touches it?"

Data speaks differently across systems. The same concept — a customer, a material, a serial number — can be represented in completely different ways in Salesforce, in legacy ERP, in Oracle CPQ, and in SAP. If you don't surface those differences during requirements gathering, they will surface during testing or cutover — at the worst possible time.

If I could go back to 2020 and tell myself one thing, it would be this: talk about data at the same time you talk about requirements. Every time. In every workshop. With every stakeholder.

Bring your data migration lead into requirements sessions. Ask business unit leaders not just what they need the system to do, but what their data actually looks like today. That conversation — uncomfortable as it sometimes is — will save you weeks of rework.

What MDG Taught Us About Governance

We implemented SAP MDG (Master Data Governance) as part of the template to manage material, vendor, and customer master data. It was valuable — but with an important caveat that I'd want anyone planning a similar program to understand.

MDG governs data going forward. It does not fix the data that already exists.

Each business unit we onboarded brought legacy master data shaped by years of independent operation. MDG's governance workflows controlled new and changed records beautifully — but the historical data still needed to be profiled, cleansed, and remediated before migration. That work had to happen alongside MDG, not instead of it.

The practical advice: activate MDG early — before business units start creating data in the new environment. If you wait until mid-program, you'll spend time cleaning up records that were created without governance in place. We learned that the hard way in early phases and adjusted for later ones.

SOX Compliance: Building It In, Not Bolting It On

Because we were building a fresh template, we had a genuine opportunity to design SOX controls correctly from the start. We used SAP GRC Access Control for Segregation of Duties across the full module landscape, with Big 4 auditors reviewing our configuration before each phase go-live.

The decisions that mattered most:

  • Design roles from scratch aligned to SoD rules — don't import legacy role conflicts into a new system
  • Configure system-enforced controls wherever possible — three-way match in MM, release strategies for purchase orders, approval workflows in FI/CO — rather than relying on manual compensating controls that auditors will question
  • Keep audit trails in SAP — not in spreadsheets, not in email chains, in the system

One thing the phased rollout surfaced repeatedly: each new business unit introduced role assignment scenarios the template hadn't anticipated. We built a GRC review checkpoint into every phase's go-live preparation. That checkpoint caught SoD conflicts before users went live rather than after — and it was worth every hour it took.

Looking Back: Was It Worth It?

Yes. Without hesitation.

What makes me say that isn't the go-live itself — it's what came after. I stayed with this client through multiple releases of this transformation. Watching the system stabilize, watching business units that had operated in silos start sharing data and collaborating through a single platform, watching the compliance posture strengthen phase by phase — that's genuinely satisfying work.

Medical device organizations exist to help patients. Better ERP systems mean better supply chain reliability, better traceability, better compliance — and ultimately better patient outcomes. That connection is real, even if it's several layers removed from the bedside.

To anyone on my team from that program who might read this: the work you did mattered. The frustrating SIT cycles, the late nights debugging integration failures, the data cleanup that nobody wanted to do — it all added up to something real.

What I'd Tell Someone Starting a Similar Program

A few honest pieces of advice from someone who has been through it:

1. Data and requirements are one conversation, not two.
Run them separately and you will regret it. Every requirement has a data reality behind it. Surface that reality early.

2. Integrations break because of data, not technology.
Invest in thorough integration testing with real, messy, edge-case data. Happy path testing will not prepare you for go-live.

3. Think organizationally, not by module.
Your stakeholders don't think in SAP modules. Neither should you when you're gathering requirements.

4. Activate MDG before people start creating data.
Governance after the fact is cleanup. Governance from the start is prevention.

5. SOX controls belong in the system.
If your control lives in a spreadsheet, it's a risk. Build it into SAP GRC and sleep better.

Final Thought

Managing this SAP S/4HANA rollout across multiple business units of a medical device company from 2020 to 2022 was one of the most challenging and rewarding programs of my career. The technical complexity was real. The data challenges were real. The pressure of SOX compliance across a phased rollout was real.

But so was the outcome.

If you're working through something similar — a multi-unit rollout, a complex integration landscape, a regulated industry environment — I'd genuinely enjoy discussing it in the comments. Every program teaches you something new. I'm still learning from this one.

entry.title: AI Product Management: A Field That Doesn't Have Its Frameworks Yet
entry.content:
Series introduction - AI Product Management for SAP Practitioners

I have been building products at SAP, at Walmart, and in regulated healthcare AI for over fifteen years. I am also a trained physician. That combination gives me an unusual vantage point on what is happening right now in AI product management, and I want to share it in a series of posts written specifically for people building on SAP's AI stack.

This is the first post. It is not a how-to. It is a map of the territory, an orientation to the problems this series will dig into, and an honest accounting of why product management for agentic AI is genuinely different from what most of us were trained to do.

The Question Nobody Asks at the Start

Here is the question that should open every agentic AI project, and almost never does: how do you design a system that is probabilistic, acts on its own, and does not know how to admit when it does not know what to do?

That question sounds abstract. It is not. Every team shipping a Joule extension, a BTP-hosted agent, or an autonomous workflow in SAP Build is answering it, whether they realize it or not. The answer is baked into their architecture choices, their evaluation frameworks, their monitoring setup, and their change management approach. Most teams answer it implicitly, by not answering it at all, and discover the problem six months after go-live when the agent is doing something nobody designed it to do.

The probabilistic part is the piece that catches people first. Traditional software is deterministic: given the same inputs, you get the same outputs. You can test it, certify it, and trust the certification. An AI system gives you a distribution of outputs. Most of the time it lands in the right part of the distribution. Sometimes it does not. The conditions that push it into the wrong part of the distribution are not always predictable in advance. This changes what testing means, what monitoring means, and what production readiness means.

The autonomous action part is what makes agentic AI qualitatively different from a recommendation engine or a classification model. An agent does not just output a suggestion for a human to act on. It acts. It calls tools, modifies records, sends communications, triggers workflows. When it is wrong, it does not just produce a wrong answer. It produces a wrong action, and wrong actions have a way of compounding before anyone notices.

The part about not admitting uncertainty is the most insidious. Large language models are fluent. They produce coherent, confident-sounding output even when they are operating outside the distribution of their training data, even when the question has no good answer, even when the right response would be to stop and ask a human. Fluency without calibration is the failure mode nobody demonstrates in a demo and everybody discovers in production.

 

The Kindergarten Problem

When my children were in kindergarten, I noticed something that has stayed with me ever since. A classroom of five-year-olds, supervised, is a manageable system. Remove the teacher for ten minutes, and you come back to a different situation than you left.

This is not a metaphor about AI being childlike. It is a structural observation about supervised versus unsupervised systems. Children in a supervised environment have access to a feedback loop: they make a choice, someone responds, they adjust. Remove the supervision and the feedback loop closes on itself. The kids are still making choices, but the signal that tells them when a choice was wrong is gone. So the choices drift.

Agentic systems behave the same way. An agent running within a well-designed supervisory loop, one that provides clear feedback, catches errors before they compound, and escalates uncertainty to human judgment, performs very differently from the same agent running without it. The model is identical. The environment is different. The outcomes diverge significantly over time.

Most enterprise AI deployments focus intensely on the agent and very little on the supervisory system around it. The evaluation is of the model. The monitoring is of the latency. The production readiness checklist covers security and compliance. Nobody asks: what does it look like when this agent has been running for six months and the humans who are supposed to supervise it have stopped paying close attention because it has been reliable? What does recovery look like when it fails in a way that the monitoring did not catch?

These are not exotic edge cases. They are the predictable outcome of systems that were designed without the supervisory layer in mind.

You Are Not a Bridge Anymore

For most of my career, the core job of a product manager was translation. You understood customer language and engineering language and you moved between them, converting benefits into capabilities and requirements into roadmap. You were the bridge.

AI has not eliminated that job. It has made it automatable. The artifacts that used to consume 60 to 70 percent of a PM's week, the PRDs, the synthesis of customer feedback, the competitive analysis, the roadmap documentation, can now be drafted in a fraction of the time. That time is not going to disappear. The question is what you do with it.

The answer I have arrived at, and that this series will explore in depth, is that the PM's job has expanded from operating the bridge to architecting the system around the bridge. You are now responsible for two things you were not responsible for before: the design of the agent's autonomy boundary (what it can decide alone, what it must escalate, what it must never do), and the design of the supervisory system around it (how humans know what the agent is doing, how they intervene when it goes wrong, how the organization maintains the judgment to catch errors it never trained for).

This is not a skill most product managers have been asked to develop. It is the skill that separates teams that deploy agentic AI successfully from teams that quietly decommission it eighteen months later.

The Autonomy Ladder

One of the frameworks I developed in writing about this topic is what I call the Autonomy Ladder. It has three rungs, and most teams are on the wrong one for the wrong reasons.

The first rung is the suggestion engine. The AI recommends and a human acts. Every decision passes through a human before anything happens. This is the safest configuration and also the most common starting point. It is also the one most teams are impatient to leave, because the efficiency gains are modest when a human has to review every output before acting on it.

The second rung is the copilot. The agent acts and a human reviews. The system takes action autonomously, but every action is logged and surfaced for human audit within a defined window. Errors are caught in retrospect rather than prevented in advance. This configuration works when the cost of a wrong action is recoverable and the volume of actions makes pre-review impractical. Most enterprise agentic deployments today are targeting this rung, and many are discovering that the audit workflow is harder to design well than the agent itself.

The third rung is the autonomous actor. The agent acts within defined boundaries with no routine human review. This is where the efficiency gains are greatest and where the governance requirements are also greatest. An agent operating here needs a precisely scoped autonomy boundary, a real-time monitoring layer that catches anomalous behavior, a clear escalation path for situations outside the boundary, and a recovery workflow for when it acts wrongly within the boundary. Most teams that reach this rung reach it before they have designed those four things.

The mistake most teams make is not choosing the wrong rung. It is moving up the ladder on a schedule rather than on demonstrated evidence that the rung below is working. Utah recently became the first US state to approve an AI agent for autonomous prescription renewals. The threshold they set for moving to autonomous operation was 250 reviewed interactions. That is a count, not a safety criterion. It says nothing about whether the agent correctly identifies the patients for whom autonomous renewal is inappropriate. The ladder was climbed on schedule, not on evidence. A future post in this series will go deep on how to earn the move up, rather than schedule it.

What This Series Covers

The posts that follow will go deep on each of these problems. The frameworks are universal; they apply to any team building agentic AI regardless of the stack. Where I have examples from the SAP ecosystem, Joule, BTP, SAP Build, and SAP Business Data Cloud, I will use them. But the problems being solved are not SAP-specific. They are the problems every product manager faces when the software they are responsible for starts taking actions in the world.

I will cover the suitability question: not every problem deserves an agent, and the evaluation framework for deciding is different from anything most PMs have used before. I will cover the cost model: the economics of agentic AI do not look like traditional software and most business cases get them wrong in predictable ways. I will cover evaluation and observability: how you measure an agent in production in a way that actually tells you whether it is working. I will cover the supervisory system design: what it means to build the human side of an agentic deployment, not just the agent. And I will cover the security layer that most teams discover too late, when an agent that passed every review has been quietly doing something nobody authorized.

This is the work. It is harder than it looks in the demo. It is also more important than most organizations currently treat it.

The agent is a powerful tool. The job of the product manager is to design the system it operates inside, not just the tool itself.

Yoram Friedman is a physician and senior product manager with 15+ years building enterprise data platforms and AI-enabled products at SAP, Walmart, and Microsoft. He writes about the intersection of AI, clinical workflow, and product design at data-decisions-and-clinics.ghost.io and publishes a guide for product managers on agentic AI.

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: Request for Bulk Partial Payment Posting Solution SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Dear Community Mentors/Members,

Good Evening

Our client requires a solution to post partial incoming payments against customer invoices in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, where the received payment clears part of the invoice, and the remaining balance automatically remains open (due) on the same invoice. They specifically need bulk upload functionality to handle this for approximately 5,000 invoices efficiently every months.

Business Context:
This scenario is common in manufacturing industries where rate amendments frequently change payment amounts. Customers make payments against multiple invoices and provide payment details (including invoice references and partial amounts) only after remittance. Users then need to post these exact received amounts as partial payments, leaving the balance due automatically on the original invoices.

Current Challenges:

  • Manual processing via F-28 or Fiori apps like "Manage Customer Line Items" is too time-consuming for 5,000+ invoices.

  • No standard app found for bulk uploading partial payments with automatic residual open items.

Request:
Please suggest if there is a standard solution for bulk posting incoming payments with partial invoice clearing (and automatic remaining balance due) in SAP Public Cloud. Possible options could include:

  • Specific Fiori apps (e.g.,"Post Incoming Payments")

  • Excel upload templates for mass payments with remaining auto due on same invoice

  • Payment file import with clearing rules with remaining auto due on same invoice

  • APIs or extensibility options for custom bulk processing

  • Any configuration for automatic partial clearing with remaining auto due on same invoice

Any relevant KBA, SAP Help Portal link, or step-by-step guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you for your assistance.

Best regards,
Chaganlal Tiwari

entry.title: AI Product Management: Seven Things You Need to Understand About AI Before You Build With It
entry.content:
Post 1 — AI Product Management series

Before the architecture review. Before the sprint planning. Seven concepts every product manager needs to have loaded before any agentic AI project begins. Not because they are advanced. Because they are prerequisites. Every design mistake I have seen in enterprise agentic deployments traces back to a team that skipped one of these.

01. Suggestion vs. Action

Label every use case. Everything downstream depends on it.

There is a categorical difference between an AI system that surfaces a recommendation and one that takes an action. A system that drafts an email and waits for the sales rep to send it is a different product, with a different accountability model and a different failure mode, than a system that sends the email autonomously. Both can be built. Neither is inherently wrong. But they require different designs, different governance, and different contracts with the user.

The mistake is treating this as a slider you can adjust later. It is a foundational decision. When a user believes the system is making suggestions and the system is actually taking actions, the trust mismatch produces exactly the kind of failure that ends up in a post-incident review. Name the contract before engineering starts. Write it into the spec. Check it at every design review.

02. Deterministic vs. Probabilistic

Know where your governance burden lives in any hybrid system.

Traditional enterprise software is deterministic: the same input produces the same output every time. You can test it with a finite set of cases, cover the relevant conditions, and trust the certification. An AI system is probabilistic: it samples from a distribution of possible outputs. The same input produces a different output on a different run. This is not a bug. It is the fundamental behavior of a system built on learned patterns rather than explicit rules.

The practical implication: most enterprise systems are hybrids. The billing rules are deterministic. The customer service agent sitting on top of them is probabilistic. The inventory system is deterministic. The demand forecasting model feeding it is probabilistic. Knowing which parts of your system are probabilistic tells you where your governance burden lives. The deterministic parts can be tested with a finite set of cases. The probabilistic parts require ongoing monitoring, statistical testing, and a fundamentally different standard of evidence for what "working" means.

A story that says "the system correctly identifies high-risk transactions" cannot have a pass/fail acceptance criterion for a probabilistic component. It needs a performance threshold, a measurement methodology, and a monitoring plan.

03. The Four-Layer Stack

Foundation model, orchestration, tools, and context. Especially context.

When your engineering team says "we're adding an AI feature," they are usually talking about the first layer. There are four.

The foundation model is the large language model from a provider. Your team selects it but does not control it. The orchestration layer is the code your team writes to coordinate agent behavior: which tools to call, in what sequence, what to do when something fails. The tool layer is every system the agent can access, every API it can call, every action it can take. The context layer is what the agent reads before it acts: the prompt, the retrieved documents, the customer record, the previous steps in the workflow.

Most teams invest heavily in layers one through three. They design layer four by accident. This is the most expensive design error in agentic AI. A model without the right context is not a useful model; it is a confident model that will fill the gaps in its knowledge with plausible-sounding inferences. Intelligence without context is just confidence.

04. Context Is the Product

Name what must travel with the data. Reject architectures that assume the agent will figure it out.

When data moves from its source system into the agent's context, it leaves things behind. A customer record travels with the account value, the ticket history, and the product usage data. What does not travel: the contractual SLA negotiated individually, the active legal review that makes anything the agent says to this customer discoverable, the "high-value" flag computed by a scoring model retired six months ago.

The data the agent sees is accurate. The context that gives the data meaning is below the waterline, invisible to the agent.

The design response is an explicit context contract: a named specification of what context must travel with the data when it moves into the agent's working memory. The question to ask at every design review is this: what does this agent need to know that is not in the data it will receive? Who owns that information? Can it be surfaced? If not, what decisions should the agent be blocked from making without it?

Architectures that assume the agent will figure out what it needs should be rejected at the design review, not discovered as a problem after the first consequential error.

05. Specificity, Sensitivity, AUC

The threshold is a PM decision, not a model setting.

For any agentic feature that makes a classification, three statistical terms determine product behavior. Sensitivity is the proportion of real positive cases the model correctly identifies. High sensitivity catches most real cases but produces more false alarms. Specificity is the proportion of true negatives the model correctly dismisses. High specificity produces fewer false alarms but misses more real cases. AUC summarizes how well the model separates positives from negatives across all threshold settings.

The threshold is the point at which the model decides to act. Lower it and the model acts more often. Raise it and the model acts less often. Your data science team cannot set it for you. Only you know what a false positive costs your users versus what a missed case costs them. A fraud model set too aggressively blocks legitimate transactions. Set too loosely, it lets fraud through. The right threshold reflects your actual cost structure, not the threshold that maximizes a statistical metric on a benchmark dataset.

This is a product decision with business consequences. Own it.

06. The Autonomy Ladder

Autonomy is earned. Define demotion triggers before launch.

In my previous post I described the three rungs: Suggestion Engine, Copilot, Autonomous Actor. The ladder matters here because it is the mechanism by which the classification in concept 01 becomes a governance commitment.

The most expensive mistake teams make is moving up the ladder on a schedule rather than on demonstrated evidence that the rung below is working. Utah recently approved an AI agent for autonomous prescription renewals after 250 reviewed interactions. That is a count, not a safety criterion. The ladder was climbed on schedule, not on evidence.

The question that should appear in every go/no-go document: what would cause us to move this agent back down a rung? Define the demotion triggers before launch, not after the first incident. The conditions that justify autonomous operation are also the conditions that, when violated, require pulling back. If you cannot name them before deployment, you do not yet have enough information to deploy.

07. Two Channels, Not One

Design the agent and the supervisory system that governs it. Both. Before you ship.

Here is a question most agentic products cannot answer: how does a supervisor intervene when the agent is doing something wrong?

Most teams do not have a good answer because they only built one channel. Channel one is the path from agent to user: outputs, recommendations, status updates, approval requests. This is the visible product, the one that gets roadmap attention and launch criteria. Channel two is the path from human supervisor back to the agent: escalation, override, configuration change, governance intervention. Channel two carries the authority flow. It is how a human takes control when something is wrong.

Without channel two, there is no real supervision. There is only observation. You can watch what the agent is doing, but you cannot do anything about it in time. A well-designed agent with no channel two is a liability waiting to scale. If you cannot interrupt the agent, override a decision, or pause an in-flight workflow, the only available response to a problem is a full shutdown. That is not supervision. That is damage followed by an emergency shutdown.

Design both channels before you ship either.

The prerequisite, not the checklist

These seven concepts are not a compliance checklist. They are the mental model the PM carries into every design decision, every sprint review, every stakeholder conversation about an agentic feature. The team that has these loaded makes different decisions at every stage, earlier, before the architectural defaults have hardened into constraints.

The next post in this series goes deeper on the first consequential decision: is this problem actually suited for an agent? Not every problem that can be solved with an agent should be. The evaluation framework is different from anything most PMs have used before.

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: Re: Key User Adaptation: Powerful New Features & Seamless Transition to Developer Extensibility
entry.content:
Hi @MK069 ,

thanks for sharing these great enhancements to UI adaptation. I have a general question wrt to this framework. You write in the entry paragraph:

With key user adaptation, you can change the user interface of SAP Fiori apps directly in the Fiori launchpad — intuitively and without having to write new code. The changes are upgrade-safe adaptations to SAP Fiori apps. Key users can open the UI Adaptation mode for a Fiori app directly from their user menu in the Fiori launchpad (menu entry Adapt UI).

We have just upgraded from S/4HANA 2023 FPS3 to S/4HANA 2025 FPS1. It seems that personal UI adaptations survived the upgrade, whereas published adaptations (made by a key user for the entire user base) did not.  We are still investigating, but I found this observation confirmed by two other SAP customers in a recent DSAG discussion (https://dsagnet.de/gremium/ui-technologien/forum/fiori-app-layout-nach-releasewechsel-upgrade-1 ).

Are published adaptations not upgrade-safe? Can they be made upgrade-safe?
Thanks for your help,
Tobias

entry.title: Re: Sales Document Approval - Standard Workflow - Single...
entry.content:
Good day @KrishnarajaBhat 

Thank you for your question.

You should control the approval through the approval request reason and the workflow start condition together. For your requirement, the approval request reason should only be set when the specific external order reason first makes the sales order relevant for finance approval. Changes you make later e.g., quantity, price, or order text should not reset or remove the approval tracking fields, and they should not trigger a new approval if the order has already passed the required financial approval.

As a next step, I suggest you do the following:

  1. Review the workflow in Manage Sales Document Workflows and confirm that the start condition matches the approval request reason set by the BAdI SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASON. See KBA 3560088 for more details.                          
  2. Review the BAdI implementation in Custom Logic and make sure the approval request reason is not set again on every save. This BAdI is called whenever a sales document is saved and setting the approval request reason triggers the approval workflow. See KBA 3086121 for more details.
  3. Make sure you test with a sales order by triggering approval once, approving it in My Inbox, then changing quantity price, or order text to confirm that a new workflow is not created.                                                                                                                   
  4. If any document is already stuck in approval and needs correction, use the standard withdrawal option in Manage Sales Orders or Manage Sales Orders Version 2. See KBA 2997066 for more insights on the steps to withdraw an approval request.                                                                                                                                                                                                         

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: France B2B e-invoicing 2026 - correct eDocument process code in DRC (FR_B2B vs FRSINV)?
entry.content:
Configuring SAP S/4HANA for the French B2B e-invoicing mandate (PA/Y-model, September 2026). Two specific questions:

1. Process code: Is FR_B2B the correct eDocument process code for outbound B2B invoices under the France PA mandate, or is FRSINV still current? We understand FRSINV may be legacy Chorus Pro / pilot. Is FR_B2B_CRN the correct type for credit notes (avoir)?

2. SAP as PA: SAP was announced as a certified Plateforme Agréée in February 2026. Does this mean customers using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance cloud edition (BTP) can transmit without contracting a separate third-party PDP? Is this limited to DRC Cloud Edition only?

Context: deploying for a customer using SAP DRC. Would be interested to hear from practitioners on both On-Premise and DRC Cloud Edition.

Any practitioner who has gone live or is in active testing for France B2B PA would be extremely helpful.

Remember, there is no such thing as "on-premise" in real English.
"Premise" is a concept.
"Premises" is a location.

"https://collectivecontent.agency/2018/04/19/should-i-say-on-premise-or-on-premises-it/"
